title: Present Perfect / Life Experiences
description: Master life experiences using the present perfect tense with ever, never, already, yet, just, and still.

lessons.title: Introducing have/has + past participle
lessons.description: Meet the present perfect tense for the first time. Learn how to form it using have/has and regular past participles.

content.title: What is the present perfect?
content.body:
The PRESENT PERFECT connects the past to the present. We use it to talk about things that happened at some point before now — without saying exactly when.

Think of it like this:
Past simple = 'I ate biryani YESTERDAY.' (specific time)
Present perfect = 'I have eaten biryani.' (at some point in my life — no specific time)

Examples:
• I have visited the Taj Mahal.
• She has finished her homework.
• We have watched that film.
• He has worked here for two years.

The time is NOT mentioned with present perfect — that is the key difference from past simple.
content.grammar_note:
Present perfect = have/has + past participle
No specific time word (yesterday, last week, in 2020)
It connects a past experience to the present

content.title: How to form the present perfect — regular verbs
content.body:
STRUCTURE:
I / You / We / They + HAVE + past participle
He / She / It + HAS + past participle

For REGULAR verbs, the past participle = past simple (-ed form):
• visit → visited     'I have visited Jaipur.'
• finish → finished   'She has finished the report.'
• work → worked      'He has worked here for years.'
• watch → watched    'They have watched the match.'
• cook → cooked      'I have cooked dal before.'
• clean → cleaned    'She has cleaned the room.'

Short forms (very common in speech):
• I have = I've
• She has = She's
• We have = We've
• He has = He's
content.grammar_note:
Regular past participle = same as past simple (-ed)
Have → I/you/we/they
Has → he/she/it
Short forms: I've, she's, we've, he's

vocabulary.meaning: something you have done or felt in your life
vocabulary.example: Travelling alone is a great experience.
She has achieved a lot in her career.

vocabulary.meaning: to do something for the first time or as a test
vocabulary.example: I have tried Chettinad food — it's spicy!
He has completed the training programme.

vocabulary.meaning: to go from one place to another
vocabulary.example: We have travelled to five states this year.
I have participated in two marathons.

sentences: I have visited Chennai three times.
She has finished her assignment.
We have watched that film before.
He has worked at this company for five years.
They have cleaned the office already.

content.question: Which sentence is in the PRESENT PERFECT tense?
content.explanation: Present perfect = have/has + past participle. No specific time word.

content.question: Choose the correct form: 'She ___ the report.'
content.explanation: She = HAS + past participle (finished). 'Has finish' is missing the -ed ending.

content.question: Which sentence uses present perfect CORRECTLY?
content.explanation: They = HAVE + past participle (watched). 'Watch' needs -ed; 'has' is wrong for they; -ing is wrong.

content.question: What is the past participle of 'work'?
content.explanation: Regular verbs: past participle = base verb + -ed. work → worked.

content.title: First time trying things — Bangalore café
content.setting: Arjun and his friend Priya are at a café. Arjun has just moved to Bangalore and they are talking about things he has and hasn't done there yet.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: So Arjun — you've been in Bangalore for a month now. Have you explored the city at all?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: A little! I have visited Lalbagh Botanical Garden — it's beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Oh lovely! And have you tried the food here? Bangalore has amazing options.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes! I have tried the masala dosa at CTR in Malleshwaram — absolutely incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: CTR is the best! What about filter coffee? Have you had it from a traditional darshini?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I have! My colleague showed me a place near Jayanagar. I have visited that area twice already.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Great! And have you shopped at Commercial Street yet?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Not yet — I have looked at it online but I haven't actually gone. I've been quite busy with work.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: We should go together this weekend! It's so much fun. Have you also visited MG Road?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes — I walked there last Sunday. I have also tried Koshy's café — I have heard about it for years.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: That's one of Bangalore's oldest restaurants! I'm glad you've already discovered some gems.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: This city is wonderful. I have enjoyed every bit of it so far!

comprehension_questions.question: Which garden has Arjun visited?
Where did Arjun try the masala dosa?
Why hasn't Arjun visited Commercial Street yet?
options: He has been busy with work.
He doesn't know where it is.
comprehension_questions.correct: He has been busy with work.

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake in each sentence.

sentences.wrong: She have finished the project.
sentences.correct: She has finished the project.
sentences.explanation: She = HAS (not have). Have is used with I/you/we/they.

sentences.wrong: I have visit Chennai three times.
sentences.correct: I have visited Chennai three times.
sentences.explanation: Present perfect needs the past participle: visit → visited.

sentences.wrong: They has watched the match.
sentences.correct: They have watched the match.
sentences.explanation: They = HAVE (not has). Has is used only with he/she/it.

content.scenario: You want to tell someone about your experience trying Kerala food at some point in your life. Which sentence is correct?
options: I tried Kerala food last month.
content.explanation: Talking about a life experience without specifying when = present perfect. 'I have tried' is correct.

content.instruction: Think about 5 things you have done in your life — places visited, food tried, activities done. Use 'I have + past participle' for each one.
content.prompt: Tell me 5 things you have done using the present perfect. Keep it simple and natural.
content.example_response: Let me share five things I have done in my life. I have visited the Golden Temple in Amritsar — it was one of the most peaceful places I have ever been. I have tried making biryani at home and it actually turned out well! I have also completed a ten-kilometre run — I trained for two months before doing it. I have learned to drive, which took me a while but I finally managed it. And I have watched every episode of a popular Indian web series — I have spent way too many weekends doing that! Each of these experiences has taught me something, so I am really glad I have done them.

content.instruction: Look at the dialogue between Arjun and Priya. Answer these questions about your own life in the same way Arjun does.
content.prompt:
Answer using present perfect:
1. Have you visited any famous places in India?
2. Have you tried any regional food from another state?
3. Have you learned any new skill recently?
content.example_response: For the first question — yes, I have visited a few famous places. I have been to the Mysore Palace and I have also visited Hampi, which was absolutely stunning. I haven't visited the Taj Mahal yet though — that's still on my list. For the second question — I have tried Rajasthani food at a restaurant in Chennai. I have eaten dal baati churma and I really enjoyed the flavours. For the third question — I have started learning digital photography in my free time. I have watched several online tutorials and I have practised quite a bit on weekends. I haven't enrolled in a formal course yet but I have made good progress on my own.

content.instruction: Complete the passage using the correct present perfect form.
content.text: Kavya ___ (1/travel) to four countries in the last two years. She ___ (2/visit) Sri Lanka, Thailand, Nepal, and the Maldives. She ___ (3/try) so many different foods along the way — she says she ___ (4/eat) the best curry of her life in Colombo. Her friend Meera ___ (5/not/travel) abroad yet, but she ___ (6/explore) many parts of India. They both ___ (7/agree) that travel changes the way you see the world.

content.title: Lesson 1 complete — Introducing have/has + past participle
key_points: Present perfect = have/has + past participle
I/you/we/they → HAVE | he/she/it → HAS
Regular past participle = verb + -ed (visit → visited)
No specific time word — talk about life experience in general
Short forms: I've, she's, he's, we've, they've
Key difference from past simple: present perfect = no specific time stated

lessons.title: Irregular past participles
lessons.description: Learn the most common irregular past participles needed for the present perfect tense.

content.title: Irregular past participles — Group 1
content.body:
Some verbs don't follow the regular -ed rule. These are called IRREGULAR verbs. You need to learn their past participles separately.

Group 1 — Base verb / Past simple / Past participle:
• go → went → GONE / BEEN
  'I have gone to the market.' (and am still there)
  'I have been to Goa.' (in my life, now back)
• see → saw → SEEN
  'I have seen that film.'
• eat → ate → EATEN
  'She has eaten already.'
• drink → drank → DRUNK
  'He has drunk three cups of chai.'
• give → gave → GIVEN
  'They have given us permission.'
content.grammar_note:
BEEN = visited and returned
GONE = went and is still there
'I've been to Kerala' (visited, back now)
'She's gone to Kerala' (she went, still there)

content.title: Irregular past participles — Group 2
content.body:
Group 2 — more common irregular past participles:
• have → had → HAD
  'I have had this phone for two years.'
• do → did → DONE
  'She has done the dishes.'
• write → wrote → WRITTEN
  'He has written three books.'
• speak → spoke → SPOKEN
  'I have spoken to the manager.'
• buy → bought → BOUGHT
  'We have bought a new car.'
• make → made → MADE
  'She has made dinner.'
• take → took → TAKEN
  'He has taken the medicine.'
• come → came → COME
  'They have come a long way.'
content.grammar_note:
bought / made / taken = same as past simple
written / spoken / done = different from past simple
Practise these by using them in sentences

vocabulary.example: I have read that book twice.
We have met before, haven't we?
I have known her for years.
I haven't slept well this week.
She has felt unwell since morning.
He has found a new job.

sentences: I have been to Kerala twice — it's beautiful.
She has done all her homework.
We have met before, haven't we?
He has written a fantastic report.
I haven't slept well for a week.

content.question: What is the past participle of 'go' when talking about a visited place (you are back now)?
content.explanation: I have BEEN to Goa = I visited and came back. I have GONE to Goa = I went and am still there.

content.question: Choose the correct sentence: 'She ___ three books this year.'
content.explanation: She = has. Write → written (irregular past participle). 'Wrote' is past simple, not past participle.

content.question: Which sentence is CORRECT?
options: I have drank two cups of coffee.
I have drink two cups of coffee.
I have drunk two cups of coffee.
I have drinking two cups of coffee.
content.correct: I have drunk two cups of coffee.
content.explanation: Drink → drank (past simple) → drunk (past participle). Present perfect uses the past participle: drunk.

content.question: What is the past participle of 'do'?
content.explanation: do → did → done. 'Did' is the past simple. 'Done' is the past participle used in present perfect.

content.title: Talking about life experiences — train journey, Mumbai to Pune
content.setting: Kavya and Sonal are strangers who meet on a train. They start talking about places they have been to and things they have done.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Excuse me — is this seat taken?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: No, please sit! Have you been on this route before?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Many times! I've done this journey at least twenty times. Have you been to Pune before?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes — I've visited twice for work. I've met some lovely people there.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: It's a great city! Have you seen the Shaniwar Wada fort?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I have! It was incredible — I had read about it before I went so I was excited. Have you been abroad at all?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I have been to Singapore once — for my cousin's wedding. I hadn't seen her in years! Have you?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I've been to Sri Lanka and Nepal. Nepal was absolutely stunning — I have never felt so small looking at the Himalayas.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I've always wanted to go! What's the best thing you have done there?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I have trekked to Nagarkot — the sunrise view was unforgettable. I have taken so many photographs!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: That sounds wonderful. I have never done a proper trek. Maybe it's time!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Absolutely — once you've done one, you'll be hooked. By the way, I'm Kavya.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'm Sonal. It's so nice to have spoken to someone interesting on this journey for once!

comprehension_questions.question: How many times has Sonal done this train journey?
Which two countries has Kavya visited?
What has Kavya done in Nepal?
options: Seen the Himalayas from a hotel

content.instruction: Find and correct the irregular past participle mistake.

sentences.wrong: I have went to Ooty many times.
sentences.correct: I have been to Ooty many times.
sentences.explanation: go → went (past simple) → been/gone (past participle). For visited places use 'been'.

sentences.wrong: She has speaked to the principal.
sentences.correct: She has spoken to the principal.
sentences.explanation: speak → spoke (past simple) → spoken (past participle). 'Speaked' is not a word.

sentences.wrong: He has buyed a new bike.
sentences.correct: He has bought a new bike.
sentences.explanation: buy → bought (past simple AND past participle). 'Buyed' doesn't exist.

content.scenario: You want to say that Priya went to the market and hasn't come back yet. Which sentence is correct?
options: Priya has been to the market.
Priya has gone to the market.
Priya have gone to the market.
Priya went to the market before.
content.correct: Priya has gone to the market.
content.explanation: GONE = went and is still there. BEEN = visited and came back. Priya hasn't returned, so 'gone' is correct.

content.instruction: Talk about things you have seen, read, eaten, or done using IRREGULAR past participles. Use at least 6 different irregular verbs.
content.prompt: Share 6 experiences using irregular past participles: seen, eaten, read, done, met, spoken, been, written, found, felt, bought, made.
content.example_response: I have seen quite a few classic Indian films — I have watched Sholay at least five times and I still enjoy it every time. I have eaten some incredible street food over the years — the pav bhaji I had in Mumbai was something I have never forgotten. I have read a few novels in English — the one I have enjoyed most is The White Tiger by Aravind Adiga. I have spoken to people from all over India because of my work, which has really helped my communication skills. I have been to Varanasi once — it was one of the most powerful experiences of my life, standing by the Ganga at sunrise. And I have met some truly wonderful people through travel. Each of these experiences has made me who I am.

content.instruction: Look at the train journey dialogue. Sonal says she has never done a proper trek. Imagine you are encouraging her. Use present perfect to talk about what trekking involves and what you have experienced.
content.prompt: Encourage a friend to try trekking. Use 'I have + irregular past participle' throughout.
content.example_response: You should definitely try it, Sonal! I have done three treks now and each one has been completely different. The first time I felt nervous because I had never done anything like it before. But once I started, I forgot about the nervousness completely. I have seen sunrises from hilltops that I would never have seen otherwise. I have met some of the most interesting people on trails — people I would never have spoken to in my normal daily life. I have also found that it changes the way you feel about your own body. After a trek, I have always felt stronger and more confident. And the food at the campsite — I have eaten the simplest meals that somehow tasted like the best food in the world because I was so hungry! Just start with an easy day trek. Once you have done your first one, you will want to do more, I promise!

content.instruction: Complete this social media bio using the correct present perfect forms.
content.text:
Hi! I'm Ravi — a traveller, foodie, and photographer based in Chennai.

I ___ (1/be) to 12 Indian states so far. I ___ (2/eat) everything from Kashmiri Wazwan to Kerala Sadya and I ___ (3/find) that every region has its own magic. I ___ (4/meet) incredible people on my journeys — some ___ (5/become) lifelong friends. I ___ (6/take) over 5,000 photographs and I ___ (7/write) about my travels on my blog. I ___ (8/not/go) abroad yet but that's next on the list! If you ___ (9/read) this far, I think we'd get along well. Let's connect!

content.title: Lesson 2 complete — Irregular past participles
key_points: Irregular verbs have special past participles — must be learned separately
go → been (visited and back) OR gone (went, still there)
Key irregulars: written, spoken, seen, eaten, drunk, done, met, read, felt, found, bought, made, taken
Past simple ≠ past participle for irregular verbs: wrote ≠ written, spoke ≠ spoken
Practice tip: say the trio aloud — go / went / been — until it feels natural
content.next_lesson: Ever and never — life experience questions

lessons.title: Ever and never — life experience questions
lessons.description: Use 'ever' and 'never' with the present perfect to ask about and describe life experiences.

content.title: Ever — asking about life experiences
content.body:
EVER means 'at any time in your life'. We use it in QUESTIONS with the present perfect.

Structure: Have/Has + subject + EVER + past participle?

Examples:
• 'Have you ever tried sushi?'
• 'Have you ever been to Rajasthan?'
• 'Has she ever spoken to a large audience?'
• 'Have they ever won a competition?'

Answering positively:
• 'Yes, I have! I tried it in Tokyo.'
• 'Yes — actually I have been there twice.'

Answering negatively:
• 'No, I haven't — but I'd love to.'
• 'No, never — it looks too adventurous for me!'
content.grammar_note:
EVER goes between the auxiliary and the past participle
Have you EVER + past participle?
Only used in questions and negatives (not 'I have ever tried')

content.title: Never — negative life experiences
content.body:
NEVER means 'at no time in my life'. We use it in NEGATIVE statements.

Structure: I/She + have/has + NEVER + past participle

Examples:
• 'I have never eaten octopus.'
• 'She has never been abroad.'
• 'We have never lost a match.'
• 'He has never tried cooking.'

NEVER makes the sentence negative — so don't add NOT:
✓ 'I have never been to Delhi.'
✗ 'I have never not been to Delhi.' (wrong — double negative)

Using never in conversation:
• 'I've never done that — what's it like?'
• 'She's never tried it but she wants to.'
content.grammar_note:
NEVER goes between have/has and the past participle
NEVER = already negative — don't add NOT
Never is stronger than 'not... yet'

vocabulary.example: Skydiving is quite an adventure!
Have you ever tried this place? I'd recommend it.

vocabulary.meaning: in or to a foreign country
vocabulary.example: Have you ever been abroad?

vocabulary.meaning: a show or presentation in front of an audience
vocabulary.example: I've never given a performance on stage.
Running a marathon is a great achievement.

vocabulary.meaning: feeling sad because something wasn't as expected
vocabulary.example: I've never been disappointed by that restaurant.

sentences: Have you ever tried Hyderabadi biryani?
Yes I have — it's absolutely incredible!
I have never been to the north of India.
Has she ever spoken in front of a large audience?
No, never — but I think she'd be great at it.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses EVER?
content.explanation: EVER goes between the auxiliary (have) and the past participle (tried). Only used in questions here.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses NEVER?
options: I have never not eaten sushi.
content.explanation: NEVER goes between have and the past participle. No extra NOT needed — NEVER is already negative.

content.question: Arjun asks: 'Have you ever been to Ladakh?' You visited once, three years ago. What do you say?
options: Yes, I went three years ago.
Yes, I have — I went three years ago. It was breathtaking!
content.correct: Yes, I have — I went three years ago. It was breathtaking!
content.explanation: Answer the present perfect question with 'Yes, I have', then naturally give more detail using past simple (when).

content.question: How do you best respond to 'Have you ever tried rock climbing?' if you haven't?
options: No, I have never tried it — but it looks exciting!
content.correct: No, I have never tried it — but it looks exciting!
content.explanation: HAVE NEVER + past participle is the correct negative form. Adding 'but it looks exciting' makes the conversation flow.

content.title: The life experience quiz — friends' gathering, Hyderabad
content.setting: At a house party, a group of friends play a 'Have you ever?' game. Each person shares experiences. The dialogue is full of ever/never patterns.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Okay everyone — let's play 'Have you ever'! I'll start. Arjun — have you ever eaten something you immediately regretted?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Ha! Yes, absolutely. I have eaten the spiciest podi dosa of my life in Madurai. I cried!

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Amazing! I have never had anything that spicy. Priya — have you ever done something that really scared you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I have! I have bungee jumped in Rishikesh. It was the most terrifying and wonderful thing I have ever done.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I have never done anything that brave — I have never even been to Rishikesh!

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: You must go! Sonal — what about you? Have you ever lived in another city?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Yes — I have lived in Bangalore, Pune, and now Hyderabad. I have never stayed in one city for more than three years.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Interesting! I have never moved cities — I have lived in Hyderabad my whole life. Have you ever regretted moving so much, Sonal?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Never! Every city has taught me something new. Have you ever learned a new language, anyone?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I have tried learning French — I have taken lessons twice but I have never finished a course!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I have actually learned basic Spanish! I have never been to a Spanish-speaking country but I have watched lots of Spanish shows.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: That's brilliant! I think we have all done some pretty interesting things. This game is great.

comprehension_questions.question: What did Priya do in Rishikesh?
Has Sonal ever regretted moving cities?
What language has Ravi tried to learn?

content.instruction: Find and correct the EVER/NEVER mistake.

sentences.wrong: I have ever visited Jaipur — it's beautiful.
sentences.correct: I have visited Jaipur — it's beautiful.
sentences.explanation: EVER is only for questions. In positive statements, just use 'I have visited' without 'ever'.

sentences.wrong: She hasn't never tried sushi.
sentences.correct: She has never tried sushi. / She hasn't tried sushi.
sentences.explanation: NEVER is already negative — don't combine hasn't + never. Double negatives are not standard in English.

sentences.wrong: Have ever you been to Mumbai?
sentences.correct: Have you ever been to Mumbai?
sentences.explanation: Word order: Have + subject + EVER + past participle. 'Have ever you' is incorrect.

content.scenario: You are meeting a new colleague from another city. You want to ask if they have tried South Indian food. Which is the most natural question?
options: Did you try South Indian food?
Do you try South Indian food?
Have you ever tried South Indian food?
You tried South Indian food ever?
content.correct: Have you ever tried South Indian food?
content.explanation: Life experience question = Have you ever + past participle? This is the standard natural form.

content.instruction: Play the 'Have you ever?' game with yourself. Ask yourself 6 questions using 'Have you ever?' and answer each one honestly with a full sentence.
content.prompt: Ask and answer 6 'Have you ever?' questions. Some answers should be YES and some NO, using NEVER.
content.example_response: Let me play the game! Have I ever been to a hill station? Yes, I have — I have been to Munnar in Kerala and the tea gardens there were absolutely breathtaking. Have I ever cooked a full meal for more than ten people? Yes, I have! Last Diwali I helped cook for around fifteen family members. Have I ever won a competition? Honestly, no — I have never won a formal competition, though I have come close a few times. Have I ever read a book in one sitting? I have! I have read a short novel in one long afternoon — I couldn't put it down. Have I ever tried learning a musical instrument? No, never — I have always wanted to learn the guitar but I have never actually started. Have I ever spoken to a complete stranger and become friends? Yes, actually — I have met some lovely people on train journeys who I have stayed in touch with!

content.instruction: Think about 3 things you have NEVER done but would like to try. Explain what they are and why you haven't done them yet.
content.prompt: Share 3 things you have never done + why + whether you'd like to try.
content.example_response: There are three things I have never done but would genuinely love to. First — I have never been to Ladakh. It's somewhere I have always dreamed of visiting but I have never had the right timing or budget to make it happen. The photographs I have seen make it look other-worldly. Second — I have never tried pottery. I have watched many videos of people doing it and it looks incredibly meditative. I have simply never found a class nearby or made the time for it. Third — I have never performed in front of an audience — not even a small one. I have always been shy about performing and I have never pushed myself to try. But I think it would be such a great confidence-builder. These are all things I genuinely hope to do — I just haven't done them yet!
key_phrases: I have always wanted to but

key_points: EVER = 'at any time in your life' — used in questions: Have you ever + past participle?
NEVER = 'at no time' — used in statements: I have never + past participle
EVER goes between have/has and the past participle

EVER is NOT used in positive statements — just in questions

lessons.title: Already and yet
lessons.description: Use 'already' and 'yet' with the present perfect to talk about tasks completed and things not done.

content.title: Already — sooner than expected
content.body:
ALREADY means something has happened — and often sooner than expected or before this moment.

We use ALREADY in POSITIVE sentences and questions.

Position: between have/has and the past participle, OR at the end.

Examples:
• 'I've already had breakfast.' (= earlier than expected)
• 'She has already submitted the report.'
• 'We've already seen that film.'
• 'Have you already finished?!' (surprise — sooner than expected)

In speech, ALREADY often shows surprise or emphasis:
• 'It's only 9 AM and he's ALREADY done all his tasks!'
content.grammar_note:
ALREADY = positive sentences and surprise questions
Position: have + ALREADY + past participle
OR: sentence + ALREADY (end position, casual)

content.title: Yet — expected but not done
content.body:
YET means something hasn't happened but is expected to happen.

We use YET in:
1. NEGATIVE sentences: 'I haven't done it yet.'
2. QUESTIONS: 'Have you done it yet?'

YET always goes at the END of the sentence.

Examples:
• 'I haven't called her yet.' (I plan to, but haven't)
• 'Have you eaten yet?' (Have you done it by now?)
• 'She hasn't arrived yet.' (We expect her but she hasn't come)
• 'Has the results been announced yet?'

Contrast:
• 'I've already done it.' (Done — finished!)
• 'I haven't done it yet.' (Not done — but will do it)
content.grammar_note:
YET goes at the END of the sentence always
Negative: haven't/hasn't + PP + YET
Question: Have + subject + PP + YET?

content.instruction: Imagine you are preparing for a big exam next week. Talk about what you have ALREADY done to prepare, and what you HAVEN'T done yet.
content.prompt: Use already and yet to describe exam preparation. Give at least 5 examples of each.
content.example_response: I have an important exam coming up next week and my preparation is quite mixed honestly! On the positive side — I have already covered the first three chapters and I have already made detailed notes for each topic. I have already done two practice papers and I have already identified my weak areas, which is the grammar section. I have already spoken to my teacher about the tricky topics and she was very helpful. However, there is still a lot left to do. I haven't practised the vocabulary exercises yet — I keep putting them off. I haven't done a full timed mock test yet, which I really need to do. I haven't revised the last two chapters yet because they are the most difficult. I also haven't organised my notes into a proper summary sheet yet. And I haven't had a proper sleep schedule yet — I have been studying late and waking up tired! I need to fix that before exam day.

content.instruction: Think about your goals or plans for this year. What have you already done? What haven't you done yet but still plan to?
content.prompt: Share your year's progress — what you've already achieved and what's still pending.
content.example_response: This year has been a mix of things achieved and things still pending! On the done side — I have already started exercising regularly, which was one of my goals. I have already read five books this year, which is more than I managed in the whole of last year. I have also already saved a decent amount each month, which feels good. On the pending side — I haven't signed up for that online course yet even though I have been thinking about it for months. I haven't taken a proper holiday yet — I have been so busy with work that I haven't booked anything. I also haven't called some old friends that I have been meaning to catch up with. And I definitely haven't started that side project I have been planning since January! Some things I have already done well but there is plenty still left to do.

key_points: ALREADY = done (before expected or before you asked) — positive sentences and surprise questions
YET = not done (but expected) — negative sentences and questions
Position: ALREADY between have/has and past participle
Position: YET always at the END of the sentence
Already: 'I've already done it!' | Yet: 'I haven't done it yet.'
Question with yet: 'Have you finished yet?' | Question with already (surprise): 'Have you already finished?!'

lessons.title: Just and still
lessons.description: Use 'just' for very recent actions and 'still' for ongoing situations that haven't changed.

content.title: Just — a very recent action
content.body:
JUST means something happened a very short time ago — moments or minutes before now.

We use JUST in POSITIVE present perfect sentences.
Position: between HAVE/HAS and the past participle.

Examples:
• 'I've just arrived.' (a moment ago)
• 'She has just called.' (moments ago)
• 'We've just eaten.' (a few minutes ago)
• 'He has just left the office.'
• 'I've just heard the news — incredible!'

JUST in conversation:
• 'Sorry — I've just had a call, can we speak in five?'
• 'Good news — she's just accepted the offer!'
content.grammar_note:
JUST = very recently — minutes or moments ago
Position: have + JUST + past participle
Used in positive statements only

content.title: Still — something that hasn't changed
content.body:
STILL means something that was expected to change hasn't changed yet — it is ongoing.

With NEGATIVE present perfect:
Subject + STILL + haven't/hasn't + past participle

Examples:
• 'She still hasn't called me back.' (I expected her to — but she hasn't)
• 'I still haven't finished the book.' (Started but ongoing)
• 'They still haven't announced the results.'
• 'He still hasn't replied to my email.'

Compare:
• 'She hasn't called yet.' = neutral — she is expected to
• 'She still hasn't called.' = with stronger expectation / slight frustration

STILL can also be used with simple present:
• 'I still live in Chennai.' (it hasn't changed)
content.grammar_note:
STILL in negative = stronger expectation than YET
Position: subject + STILL + haven't/hasn't + PP
Slightly more frustration or emphasis than YET

content.explanation: STILL adds emphasis — it suggests you really expected it to have happened by now. More feeling than YET.

content.instruction: Find and correct the JUST or STILL mistake.

sentences.correct: He still hasn't replied.

content.instruction: You've had a very eventful day. Tell someone about 5 things that have JUST happened and 3 things you STILL haven't done.
content.prompt: Describe recent events (just) and ongoing gaps (still haven't) from your day.
content.example_response: What a day it's been! Let me catch you up. I've just come back from a really tough meeting — the client changed all their requirements at the last minute. I've just had a coffee though so I'm feeling a bit more human! I've just also got a message from my friend Priya saying she's back in Chennai this weekend, which is exciting. And my manager has just approved my leave request — I wasn't expecting that so quickly. Oh, and the new team member has just sent her first draft for review. On the pending side though — I still haven't finished the project report that was due yesterday. I still haven't replied to three emails from this morning because the meetings kept interrupting. And I still haven't eaten anything since breakfast — I completely forgot about lunch with all the chaos! Tomorrow will hopefully be calmer.

content.instruction: Give an update to a friend who has asked how a particular situation in your life is going. Use JUST and STILL to describe what has recently happened and what is ongoing.
content.prompt: Give a life update — a project, a health situation, a job search, a personal goal — using just and still haven't.
content.example_response: So you asked about my job search — let me give you the full update! I've just had my third interview this week — it was for a company in Bangalore and it went really well. I've just sent them a follow-up email this afternoon. But I still haven't heard back from the first company I interviewed with two weeks ago — I'm not sure if that means a no or they are just very slow. I've just finished updating my LinkedIn profile as well, which was long overdue. My resume looks much better now. I still haven't decided whether to apply for the Hyderabad role — it seems good but it means moving cities. I've just been doing a lot of thinking about that. The good news is I've just been recommended by a former colleague for a role at her company, which is very exciting. So progress is happening — slowly but surely!

content.title: Lesson 5 complete — Just and still
key_points: JUST = very recently (moments ago) — positive present perfect sentences
STILL = ongoing, hasn't changed yet — negative present perfect (still + haven't/hasn't)
Position: JUST between have/has and past participle
Position: STILL before haven't/hasn't
STILL shows stronger expectation than YET — often slight frustration
Contrast: 'I've just done it' (done moments ago) vs 'I still haven't done it' (ongoing)
content.next_lesson: Present perfect negatives — haven't and hasn't

lessons.title: Present perfect negatives — haven't and hasn't
lessons.description: Form and use negative present perfect sentences to talk about things that have not happened.

content.title: Forming the negative — haven't and hasn't
content.body:
To make a NEGATIVE present perfect sentence, add NOT after have/has:

have not = HAVEN'T
has not = HASN'T

Structure:
I / You / We / They + HAVEN'T + past participle
He / She / It + HASN'T + past participle

Examples:
• 'I haven't eaten lunch yet.'
• 'She hasn't called back.'
• 'We haven't seen that film.'
• 'He hasn't finished the report.'
• 'They haven't arrived yet.'

Full forms (more formal/emphatic):
• 'I have not received any reply.'
• 'She has not submitted the form.'
content.grammar_note:
haven't = have not (I/you/we/they)
hasn't = has not (he/she/it)
Full forms used for emphasis or formal writing

content.title: Common contexts for negative present perfect
content.body:
We use the negative present perfect in many everyday situations:

Tasks not done:
• 'I haven't finished the assignment.'
• 'She hasn't booked the tickets yet.'

Experiences not had:
• 'I haven't been to North India.'
• 'He hasn't tried Korean food.'

Expected things that didn't happen:
• 'They haven't replied to our email.'
• 'The doctor hasn't called back yet.'

Useful combinations:
• haven't + yet = expected but not done
• still + haven't = stronger expectation
• haven't + ever = never in my life
content.grammar_note:
haven't yet = expected soon
still haven't = been waiting / frustrated
haven't ever = same as never

content.title: Vocabulary — things not done or experienced

vocabulary.meaning: to succeed in doing something difficult
vocabulary.example: I haven't managed to fix it yet.

vocabulary.meaning: to find time to do something
vocabulary.example: I haven't got round to calling her yet.

vocabulary.meaning: to make the effort to do something
vocabulary.example: He hasn't bothered to reply.
She hasn't attempted the last question.

vocabulary.meaning: to handle a situation or task
vocabulary.example: I haven't dealt with those emails yet.
He hasn't got back to me yet.

sentences: I haven't finished the report yet — give me an hour.
She hasn't called back — I'm still waiting.
We haven't been to Rajasthan — it's on our list.
He hasn't got back to me about the meeting time.
They haven't replied to the proposal yet.

content.example_response: There are three places I haven't visited yet that are really on my list. The first is Ladakh — I haven't been there because it requires good planning and the right season, and I haven't managed to get both of those things right at the same time. But the photographs I have seen are extraordinary — the landscapes look like another planet. key_points: HAVEN'T = have not — used with I/you/we/they
HASN'T = has not — used with he/she/it
Structure: subject + haven't/hasn't + past participle

lessons.title: Present perfect questions — Have you...? Has she...?
lessons.description: Form and use present perfect questions naturally in conversation, with correct short answers and follow-ups.

content.title: Forming present perfect questions
content.body:
To make a QUESTION in the present perfect, move HAVE/HAS to the front:

Structure: HAVE/HAS + subject + past participle?

Examples:
• 'Have you eaten?' (I/you/we/they → Have)
• 'Has she called?' (he/she/it → Has)
• 'Have they arrived yet?'
• 'Has the parcel come?'
• 'Have you ever been to Delhi?'
• 'Has he finished the report?'

WH- questions:
• 'What have you done today?'
• 'Where has she gone?'
• 'How long have you worked here?'
• 'Why haven't they replied?'
content.grammar_note:
Question: HAVE/HAS + subject + past participle?
WH- question: WH- word + have/has + subject + past participle?
No DO/DID in present perfect questions

content.title: Short answers and natural follow-ups
content.body:
SHORT ANSWERS:
• 'Have you eaten?' → 'Yes, I have.' / 'No, I haven't.'
• 'Has she called?' → 'Yes, she has.' / 'No, she hasn't.'
• 'Have they arrived?' → 'Yes, they have.' / 'No, not yet.'

NATURAL FOLLOW-UPS after YES:
Present perfect question → Past simple for details (when, where, how)
• 'Have you been to Goa?' — 'Yes, I have! I went last March.'
• 'Has she tried the new place?' — 'Yes — she went yesterday and loved it.'

This is very important — the present perfect opens the topic, past simple gives the detail.
content.grammar_note:
Short answer: Yes, I have. / No, I haven't.
NEVER: Yes, I have eaten. (too long for short answer)
Follow up with past simple for specific details

content.next_lesson: Present perfect vs past simple — the key difference

lessons.title: Present perfect vs past simple — when to use each
lessons.description: Understand the most important distinction in English grammar — when to use the present perfect and when to use the past simple.

content.title: The key difference — specific time or not?
content.body:
This is one of the most important grammar rules in English:

PRESENT PERFECT → No specific time mentioned
• 'I have seen that film.' (at some point — we don't say when)
• 'She has visited Kerala.' (in her life — time not important)

PAST SIMPLE → Specific time is mentioned OR the time is clearly finished
• 'I saw that film last week.' (specific — last week)
• 'She visited Kerala in 2019.' (specific year)
• 'He called me this morning.' (this morning = finished time)

The golden rule:
If you say WHEN → use past simple
If you DON'T say when → often use present perfect
content.grammar_note:
Specific time (yesterday, last year, in 2020, at 3PM) → PAST SIMPLE
No specific time (ever, never, already, yet, just) → PRESENT PERFECT
Time words that trigger past simple: ago, yesterday, last, in [year]

content.title: Time signal words — which tense do they trigger?
content.body:
PRESENT PERFECT time signals:
• ever / never
• already / yet / just / still
• recently / lately
• so far / up to now
• for + duration (for 3 years)
• since + start point (since Monday)
• today / this week / this year (if still ongoing)

PAST SIMPLE time signals:
• yesterday
• last week / last month / last year
• ago (two days ago, a year ago)
• in + year (in 2020)
• on + day (on Monday)
• at + time (at 3 PM)
• when I was a child / when I was young
content.grammar_note:
TODAY is tricky — use past simple if the day is over, present perfect if still ongoing
This year = present perfect (year not finished)
Last year = past simple (year is over)

vocabulary.meaning: not long ago, in the recent past
vocabulary.example: I've recently started a new course. (PP)

vocabulary.meaning: a period of time before now
vocabulary.example: I started that course two years ago. (PS)

vocabulary.meaning: up to this point in time
vocabulary.example: I've finished three chapters so far. (PP)

vocabulary.meaning: the most recent one (last week / last month)
vocabulary.example: I went to Goa last summer. (PS)
She called just now. (PS — very immediate)
I've had two coffees this morning. (PP if morning ongoing)

content.explanation: 'In 2018' is a specific time → PAST SIMPLE. 'Visited' is correct.

content.question: 'She ___ the Taj Mahal — it's on her bucket list.' Which fits?
content.explanation: No specific time mentioned — life experience so far → PRESENT PERFECT NEGATIVE (hasn't visited).

content.explanation: 'Yesterday' = specific finished time → PAST SIMPLE (saw). Never use present perfect with 'yesterday'.

content.question: '___ you ever tried Kashmiri food?' — Which is correct?
content.explanation: 'Ever' = life experience question → PRESENT PERFECT. Have + you + ever + past participle?

content.title: Talking about food experiences — food blogger interview, Mumbai
content.setting: A journalist is interviewing Kavya, a popular food blogger. The interview is full of natural present perfect and past simple switching.

dialogue.speaker: Journalist
dialogue.text: Kavya, you're a well-known food blogger. Have you always loved food?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Always! I grew up in a family where food was very important. My grandmother was an incredible cook.

dialogue.speaker: Journalist
dialogue.text: How many restaurants have you reviewed so far?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I've reviewed over 300 restaurants across India. I started the blog five years ago and I've been at it ever since.

dialogue.text: Which city has the best food in your experience?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Oh yes! Once I had food poisoning at a fancy restaurant in Delhi. I spent two days recovering. I've never returned there!

dialogue.text: Have you been abroad for food research?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya

dialogue.text: What's the most unusual thing you've ever eaten?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya

dialogue.speaker: Kavya

content.instruction: Find and correct the tense mistake.

sentences.wrong: I have seen him yesterday at the market.
sentences.correct: I saw him yesterday at the market.
sentences.explanation: 'Yesterday' = specific finished time → PAST SIMPLE. Never use present perfect with 'yesterday'.

sentences.wrong: She worked here for five years — she's still here.
sentences.correct: She has worked here for five years — she's still here.
sentences.explanation: She is STILL here — the action continues to now → PRESENT PERFECT (has worked).

sentences.wrong: Have you been to Goa last summer?
sentences.correct: Did you go to Goa last summer?
sentences.explanation: 'Last summer' = specific finished time → PAST SIMPLE. Present perfect cannot be used with 'last'.

content.instruction: Talk about your travel or food experiences using BOTH tenses correctly. Use present perfect for the general experience, then past simple for specific details.
content.prompt: Describe 3 experiences — use present perfect to introduce, past simple to give details.
content.example_response: Let me share three experiences using both tenses correctly. First — I have been to Munnar twice. The first time I went was in 2019 with my college friends. We drove up from Kochi and the journey took about four hours. The tea gardens were absolutely stunning. The second time I went was last year with my family. On that trip we also visited Eravikulam National Park. Second — I have tried making pasta from scratch. I attempted it for the first time about six months ago, following a recipe online. It took much longer than I expected and the pasta came out a bit uneven, but it tasted good! I've made it twice since then and it gets easier each time. Third — I have read the complete Harry Potter series. I started reading it when I was twelve years old. I finished the final book in about two weeks — I couldn't put it down. I have re-read certain parts many times since then.

The second time was last year — I felt I had grown as much as I could in that role. Both decisions were good ones. Second — I have been learning watercolour painting recently. For the fourth — yes, I have made a decision I regretted. I turned down a really good job opportunity about three years ago because I was too comfortable where I was. I ran it for the first time on Tuesday morning and it was lovely — along a lake I didn't even know existed.

content.instruction: Complete this blog post using present perfect or past simple.
content.text:
My Coffee Journey

I ___ (1/love) coffee for as long as I can remember. Growing up, my grandfather ___ (2/make) the most incredible filter coffee every morning. I ___ (3/try) many different types of coffee since then. Last year, I ___ (4/visit) a coffee estate in Coorg and it completely changed the way I think about coffee. I ___ (5/never/realise) how much goes into a single cup before that trip. Since then, I ___ (6/start) learning about different brewing methods. Last month, I ___ (7/buy) my first pour-over coffee set and I ___ (8/practise) with it every morning since. I ___ (9/not/perfect) my technique yet but the results are already much better than what I ___ (10/drink) before. Coffee is a journey, not a destination!

content.title: Lesson 8 complete — Present perfect vs past simple
key_points: PAST SIMPLE = specific finished time (yesterday, last year, in 2019, ago)
PRESENT PERFECT = no specific time / life experience / still ongoing
Key rule: If you mention WHEN → past simple. If you don't → often present perfect
PP time signals: ever, never, already, yet, just, recently, so far, for, since
PS time signals: yesterday, last, ago, in [year], on Monday, at 3 PM
Pattern in conversation: PP to introduce → PS to give specific detail
content.next_lesson: For and since — duration with present perfect

lessons.title: For and since — duration with the present perfect
lessons.description: Use 'for' and 'since' correctly with the present perfect to talk about how long something has been happening.

content.title: For — a period of time
content.body:
FOR tells us the LENGTH or DURATION of time something has been happening.

FOR + period of time:
• for two years
• for six months
• for a long time
• for three weeks
• for ages
• for my whole life

Examples:
• 'I have lived in Chennai for ten years.'
• 'She has worked here for six months.'
• 'We have been friends for a long time.'
• 'He has had this phone for two years.'

Test: Can you replace it with 'a period of ___'? → Use FOR
content.grammar_note:
FOR = duration (how long)
FOR + period: for two days, for years, for ages
Used with present perfect for ongoing situations

content.title: Since — a starting point
content.body:
SINCE tells us the STARTING POINT — when something began.

SINCE + point in time:
• since Monday
• since 2018
• since January
• since I was a child
• since this morning
• since we last met

Examples:
• 'I have lived here since 2015.'
• 'She has worked here since March.'
• 'He hasn't called since last Thursday.'
• 'We have been friends since school.'

Test: Can you replace it with 'starting from ___'? → Use SINCE
content.grammar_note:
SINCE = start point (when it began)
SINCE + point in time: since Monday, since 2020
Since is always used with present perfect (not past simple)

vocabulary.example: I have studied English for two years.
I have studied English since 2022.
I haven't seen her for ages!

vocabulary.meaning: from when I was a child
vocabulary.example: I have loved music since childhood.
I have been waiting for a while.
We met in 2019 and have been friends since then.

sentences: I have lived in Bangalore for three years.
She has worked at this company since 2020.
We have been friends since school — that's almost fifteen years!
He hasn't called me for two weeks.
I have loved cricket since I was seven years old.

content.question: 'She has worked here ___ six months.' Which word fits?
content.explanation: 'Six months' is a period of time (duration) → FOR. 'Since' needs a specific starting point like 'January'.

content.question: 'I have known her ___ we were in school together.' Which word fits?
content.explanation: 'We were in school' is a starting point in time → SINCE. It's not a duration, it's when it began.

content.question: Which sentence is CORRECT?
options: I have lived here since three years.
I have lived here for three years.
I have lived here since three years ago.
content.correct: I have lived here for three years.
content.explanation: THREE YEARS = duration → FOR. Since needs a specific date or point: 'since 2020' OR 'for three years'.

content.question: How do you ask about duration? 'How long ___ you ___ here?'
content.explanation: How long = asking about duration of an ongoing situation → PRESENT PERFECT: How long HAVE you LIVED here?

content.instruction: Fill in with FOR or SINCE.

sentences.text: I have studied English ___ three years.
She has been a doctor ___ 2015.
We haven't seen each other ___ ages.
He has lived in this flat ___ he graduated.
I haven't eaten anything ___ this morning.

sentences.text: Priya ___ worked at TCS ___ five years now.
I ___ known Ravi ___ we were in college together.

content.answer: I have lived in Chennai for ten years.

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct SINCE question.
content.answer: How long have you known her?

content.instruction: Match the time expression to FOR or SINCE.

content.title: Old friends catch up — Hyderabad coffee shop
content.setting: Meera and Sonal meet after a long gap. They talk about how long things have been going on in their lives.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Sonal! I haven't seen you for so long! How have you been?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I've been good — really busy though. I've been at my new job since February. It's been intense!

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Oh! How long have you been there?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: About eight months now. And how long have you been in Hyderabad? Last I heard you were in Pune.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I moved here two years ago! I've been here since 2022. I love the city — the food especially.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: The biryani here is legendary. Have you been going to good restaurants?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I've been a regular at this one place since my first week here! How are your parents? I haven't heard about them for ages.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: They're well — my father has been retired for three years now and he's really enjoying it. My mother has been learning yoga since last year.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: How lovely! And what about Arjun — you've been together for a while now, haven't you?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Yes — four years in December! We've been together since our first job. Time flies!

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Doesn't it! I've known you since college — that's almost twelve years.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Twelve years! And you've been one of my best friends for every single one of them.

comprehension_questions.question: How long has Sonal been at her new job?
Since when has Meera been in Hyderabad?
How long have Sonal and Arjun been together?

content.instruction: Find and correct the FOR/SINCE mistake.

sentences.wrong: I have lived here since five years.
sentences.correct: I have lived here for five years.
sentences.explanation: FIVE YEARS is a duration → FOR. Since needs a specific starting point.

sentences.wrong: She has worked here for January.
sentences.correct: She has worked here since January.
sentences.explanation: JANUARY is a starting point in time → SINCE. For needs a duration like 'for three months'.

sentences.wrong: How long have you know her?
sentences.correct: How long have you known her?
sentences.explanation: Present perfect needs the PAST PARTICIPLE: known (not the base verb 'know').

content.scenario: Someone asks: 'How long have you been learning English?' You started in 2021. What do you say?
options: I have been learning English since 2021.
I have been learning English since three years.
content.correct: I have been learning English since 2021.
content.explanation: 2021 is a starting point → SINCE. Present perfect continuous (have been learning) is natural for ongoing activities.

content.instruction: Talk about 5 things in your life that have been ongoing — how long they have lasted. Use FOR and SINCE correctly.
content.prompt: Share 5 ongoing situations using for and since.
content.example_response: Let me share five things that have been ongoing in my life. First — I have lived in my current flat for about two years now. We moved in just after the pandemic and I have really settled in well. Second — I have had my current phone for almost three years — it's still working perfectly so I haven't felt the need to change it. Third — I have been a member of the local library since 2020. I joined during the lockdown when I started reading more seriously. Fourth — I have worked at my current company since January of this year. It has been a steep learning curve but very rewarding. And fifth — I have been learning to play the ukulele for about six months. My flatmate plays guitar and inspired me to try a string instrument. I haven't become very good yet but I am enjoying the process!
key_phrases: I have been a member since

content.instruction: Interview someone about their life using 'How long have you...?' questions. Ask 5 questions and give imagined or real answers.
content.prompt: Ask and answer 5 'How long have you...?' questions covering work, home, hobbies, and relationships.
content.example_response: Let me do a self-interview! First question — how long have I lived in Chennai? I have lived here my whole life — over twenty-five years now. It's home. Second — how long have I worked in my current field? I have worked in marketing for about four years, since I graduated from college. Third — how long have I played cricket? I have played since I was about eight years old. My father taught me and I have loved the sport ever since. Fourth — how long have I been interested in cooking? I have been interested in cooking since I was a teenager. My mother is an excellent cook and I used to watch her all the time. Fifth — how long have I known my best friend? I have known him for over fifteen years — we met in school and have been close ever since. These are the things that have shaped who I am.

content.instruction: Complete this profile using for or since.
content.text:
Name: Ravi Sharma
Profession: Software Engineer

Ravi has worked at his current company ___ (1) three years. He joined ___ (2) 2021 after completing his master's degree. He has been interested in coding ___ (3) he was fourteen years old. Ravi has lived in Bangalore ___ (4) his graduation — before that, he lived in Jaipur ___ (5) most of his life. He has been playing tennis ___ (6) about two years. He started playing ___ (7) he moved to a flat with a sports complex nearby. He has been with his girlfriend ___ (8) five years — they met at university and have been together ___ (9) then. Ravi has not been back to his hometown ___ (10) almost a year — he misses his family.

content.title: Lesson 9 complete — For and since
key_points: FOR = duration (how long): for three years, for ages, for a long time
SINCE = starting point (when it began): since Monday, since 2020, since childhood
Both are used with PRESENT PERFECT for ongoing situations
Test: duration? → FOR | specific start point? → SINCE
Common mistake: 'since three years' is wrong — use 'for three years'
How long question: How long have you + past participle?
content.next_lesson: How much and how many — quantity language

lessons.title: How much and how many — quantity review
lessons.description: Review countable and uncountable nouns and use how much, how many, a lot of, much, and many correctly.

content.title: Countable vs uncountable — quick review
content.body:
COUNTABLE nouns can be counted — they have a singular and plural:
• one book / two books
• a cup / three cups
• an apple / five apples
• a friend / many friends

UNCOUNTABLE nouns cannot be counted — no plural form:
• water (not waters)
• money (not moneys)
• information (not informations)
• advice (not advices)
• traffic (not traffics)
• rice (not rices)
• weather (not weathers)
• time (not times — in this sense)

KEY RULE: Uncountable nouns are always SINGULAR → use is, has, was
content.grammar_note:
Uncountable: water, money, time, rice, information, advice, news, traffic, weather, music
Countable: book, car, person, idea, friend, problem
Uncountable = singular verb: The water IS cold.

content.title: How much / How many / A lot of
content.body:
HOW MUCH → uncountable nouns (and prices)
• 'How much water do you drink per day?'
• 'How much does this cost?'
• 'How much time do we have?'

HOW MANY → countable nouns (plural)
• 'How many books have you read this year?'
• 'How many people are coming?'
• 'How many cups of chai have you had today?'

A LOT OF → both countable AND uncountable (positive)
• 'I drink a lot of water.'
• 'I have a lot of friends here.'

MUCH → uncountable (negative + questions mainly)
• 'I don't have much time.'

MANY → countable (negative + questions mainly)
• 'Not many people came.'
content.grammar_note:
HOW MUCH + uncountable
HOW MANY + countable plural
A LOT OF = both (positive sentences)
MUCH/MANY = mainly negative and questions

content.title: Vocabulary — common countable and uncountable nouns

vocabulary.example: There is so much traffic on OMR today.
How much luggage are you bringing?
We haven't bought much furniture yet.
She has a lot of knowledge in this area.
How many complaints have we received this week?
How many suggestions did the team make?

sentences: How much water do you drink every day?
How many people are joining the team next month?
I drink a lot of coffee — probably too much!
Not many students passed the test this time.
There isn't much time left — we need to hurry.

content.question: '___ money do you have with you?' Which word fits?
content.explanation: Money is UNCOUNTABLE → HOW MUCH. You can't say 'how many money'.

content.question: '___ books have you read this year?' Which fits?
content.explanation: Books = COUNTABLE → HOW MANY. You can count books: one book, two books.

content.question: Which sentence is CORRECT?
options: I have many informations about this.
I have a lot of information about this.
I have much informations about this.
I have a lot informations about this.
content.correct: I have a lot of information about this.
content.explanation: Information = UNCOUNTABLE → never plural, never 'many'. Use 'a lot of information'.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses MUCH in a negative?
options: I don't have much time before my meeting.
content.correct: I don't have much time before my meeting.
content.explanation: Time = uncountable → don't have much time. MUCH is used with uncountable nouns.

content.instruction: Fill in with HOW MUCH or HOW MANY.

sentences.text: ___ sugar do you take in your coffee?
___ people live in your building?
___ luggage can I bring on the flight?
___ meetings do you have today?
___ traffic was there on the highway?

content.instruction: Fill in with A LOT OF, MUCH, or MANY.

sentences.text: I don't have ___ time — can we make this quick?
She has ___ experience in this field — she's been doing it for years.
Not ___ students attended the optional workshop.
There is ___ traffic on Anna Salai every morning.
I don't know ___ people in this city — I've only just moved here.

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct quantity question.

content.instruction: Match the noun to the correct question word.

content.title: Planning a road trip — Chennai
content.setting: Priya and Arjun are planning a road trip. Their conversation is full of quantity questions using how much, how many, and related language.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: So Arjun — how many people are joining the road trip?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Six people including us. How much luggage is everyone bringing?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I've told everyone — one bag each! How much space does your car have?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Plenty for six people and six bags. How many nights are we staying?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Three nights. How much is the accommodation per night?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: About two thousand each — so six thousand total per person. How much money should we each bring?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'd say at least ten thousand — for food, fuel, and activities. How many activities have you planned?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Not many — just two or three. I want to leave a lot of time to explore freely.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Good idea. How much petrol does the drive take?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: About two full tanks each way. Not too much. How many snacks are we packing?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: A lot! I've already bought a lot of snacks. How much water should we bring?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Get at least six litres — it's going to be warm. Not much else to sort — I think we're ready!

comprehension_questions.question: How many people are going on the road trip?
How much does accommodation cost per person per night?
How many activities has Arjun planned?

content.instruction: Find and correct the quantity mistake.

sentences.wrong: How many money do you have?
sentences.correct: How much money do you have?
sentences.explanation: Money is UNCOUNTABLE → HOW MUCH (not how many).

sentences.wrong: I have many informations about this topic.
sentences.correct: I have a lot of information about this topic.
sentences.explanation: Information is UNCOUNTABLE → no plural, use 'a lot of information' not 'many informations'.

sentences.wrong: She doesn't have many luggage.
sentences.correct: She doesn't have much luggage.
sentences.explanation: Luggage is UNCOUNTABLE → MUCH (not many) in negatives.

content.scenario: Your colleague asks about the feedback from last week's presentation. You received a lot of positive comments. What do you say?
options: We received a lot of positive feedback.
content.correct: We received a lot of positive feedback.
content.explanation: Feedback is UNCOUNTABLE → no plural. Use 'a lot of feedback' in positive sentences.

content.instruction: Talk about your lifestyle using how much and how many questions — then answer them about yourself.
content.prompt: Ask yourself 6 questions using how much and how many, then answer each one honestly.
content.example_response: Let me ask and answer some lifestyle questions! How much water do I drink per day? Honestly, not as much as I should — probably only four to five glasses, when I know eight is recommended. How many hours of sleep do I get? About seven on weekdays, more on weekends. How much exercise do I do? Not much at all during the week — I've been meaning to fix this. Maybe twenty minutes of walking at best. How many meals do I eat per day? Usually three — breakfast, lunch, and dinner — plus quite a lot of snacks! How much social media time do I spend? Too much, honestly. At least two hours a day which feels excessive. And how many books have I read this year? Seven so far — which I'm actually proud of. I read a lot of non-fiction this year. Some habits are good and some I'm working on!

content.instruction: You are helping to plan a company celebration for 50 employees. Discuss what you need using how much and how many language.
content.prompt: Plan a company event — use how much and how many to discuss quantities of food, space, budget, etc.
content.example_response: Planning a celebration for fifty people is quite an undertaking! Let me think through the key questions. First — how many tables will we need? With fifty people and groups of eight, that's about six to seven tables. How many chairs of course matches the number of people. How much food will we need? A lot — for a sit-down dinner, typically about 200 to 250 grams of food per person. How much will the catering cost? If we budget around 500 rupees per head, that's 25,000 rupees for food alone. How many vegetarian and non-vegetarian options do we need? I'd say we need at least three vegetarian options as a lot of people may prefer it. How much decoration budget should we keep? Maybe 5,000 rupees — not much but enough for some balloons, flowers, and a backdrop. How many staff will we need for the event? Probably four to five people serving. And how much time do we need to set everything up — at least three hours before the event. I think with good planning, this can be done really well!

content.instruction: Complete this nutrition advice article using HOW MUCH, HOW MANY, A LOT OF, MUCH, or MANY.
content.text: Eating well doesn't have to be complicated. First — ___ (1) water should you drink? Experts recommend at least eight glasses a day. Not ___ (2) people manage this, but it makes a huge difference. How ___ (3) vegetables should you eat? At least five portions a day. A lot of people don't eat ___ (4) vegetables — they eat too ___ (5) processed food instead. How ___ (6) protein do you need? About 50 to 60 grams for most adults. There is ___ (7) confusion about this topic, but the basics are simple. How ___ (8) meals should you eat? Three balanced meals with not too ___ (9) snacking in between. And how ___ (10) sugar do you consume? Most of us eat far too much — try cutting down gradually.

content.title: Lesson 10 complete — How much and how many
key_points: HOW MUCH + uncountable (water, money, time, traffic, information)
HOW MANY + countable plural (books, people, suggestions, complaints)
A LOT OF = works with both countable and uncountable (positive sentences)
MUCH = mainly negatives and questions with uncountable
MANY = mainly negatives and questions with countable
Uncountable nouns: no plural form, always singular verb
content.next_lesson: Too much, too many and enough

lessons.title: Too much, too many and enough
lessons.description: Use too much, too many, and enough to describe problems and sufficiency in everyday situations.

content.title: Too much and too many — more than needed
content.body:
TOO MUCH and TOO MANY both mean 'more than is needed or acceptable'. The difference is the noun type:

TOO MUCH + uncountable noun:
• 'There's too much sugar in this chai.'
• 'I have too much work this week.'
• 'She spends too much money on clothes.'
• 'There is too much traffic on this road.'

TOO MANY + countable noun (plural):
• 'There are too many people in this room.'
• 'I have too many meetings today.'
• 'She bought too many vegetables — they'll spoil.'
• 'There are too many errors in this document.'

TOO always shows a PROBLEM — something is excessive.
content.grammar_note:
TOO MUCH + uncountable
TOO MANY + countable plural
TOO = excessive (negative meaning — a problem)
Too much work = more than I can handle

content.title: Enough — the right amount
content.body:
ENOUGH means 'as much/many as needed' — the right quantity.

With NOUNS: enough + noun
• 'I have enough money to buy this.'
• 'Is there enough food for everyone?'
• 'We don't have enough chairs.'
• 'There is enough time, don't rush.'

With ADJECTIVES: adjective + enough
• 'Is the water hot enough?'
• 'She is experienced enough for the role.'
• 'I'm not tall enough to reach that shelf.'

NOT ENOUGH = insufficient:
• 'There isn't enough space in this room.'
• 'He doesn't have enough experience.'
content.grammar_note:
ENOUGH + noun: enough time, enough space
Adjective + ENOUGH: big enough, fast enough
NOT ENOUGH = insufficient / less than needed

vocabulary.meaning: having too much to deal with
vocabulary.example: I'm overwhelmed — there's too much to do.
There's a shortage of parking spaces.
An excess of sugar is bad for your health.
Is the budget sufficient for this project?
The preparation was adequate but not excellent.
Parking is scarce in this area.

sentences: There are too many people here — let's find a quieter table.
I've had too much coffee today — I can't sleep.
Is there enough food for everyone?
She doesn't have enough experience for the senior role.
We didn't have enough time to finish the project properly.

content.question: 'I have ___ meetings today — I don't have time for anything else.' Which fits?
content.explanation: Meetings = countable → TOO MANY. 'Too much' is for uncountable nouns.

content.question: 'She didn't get the job — she isn't experienced ___.' Which fits?
content.explanation: Adjective + ENOUGH: 'experienced enough'. The adjective comes BEFORE enough.

content.question: Which sentence uses ENOUGH correctly?
options: There is time enough for a break.
There is enough time for a break.
There is enough of time for a break.
There is time for enough a break.
content.correct: There is enough time for a break.
content.explanation: ENOUGH + noun: enough TIME. Enough comes BEFORE the noun.

content.question: 'There's ___ sugar in this drink — it's overpowering.' Which fits?
content.explanation: Sugar = uncountable → TOO MUCH. It means excessive, more than acceptable.

content.instruction: Fill in with TOO MUCH, TOO MANY, or ENOUGH.

sentences.text: I can't take another project — I have ___ work already.
We have ___ chairs — we need three more for the guests.
There were ___ cars in the car park — we had to park outside.
Is there ___ rice for everyone? I think we need to cook more.
